questions.question: What is the birthday attack bound for finding a collision in an n-bit hash function?
options: 2^n hash evaluations — same as brute force
2^(n/2) hash evaluations — due to the birthday paradox
2^(n-1) hash evaluations — you need nearly the full space
n * 2^(n/2) hash evaluations — linear in the output size
questions.explanation: The birthday paradox shows that among 2^(n/2) random values from a space of 2^n, there's roughly a 50% chance of a collision. This means SHA-256 has only 128-bit collision resistance, not 256-bit. This is the fundamental reason we use 256-bit hashes when we want 128-bit security.

questions.question: Which property of a hash function means: given a hash value h, it is computationally infeasible to find any message m such that H(m) = h?
options: Collision resistance
Second preimage resistance
Preimage resistance
Avalanche effect
questions.explanation: Preimage resistance means you cannot reverse the hash function — given only the output h, finding any input that produces it requires ~2^n work. Collision resistance (finding any two inputs with the same hash) is a different and stronger property. Second preimage resistance is in between: given one input, finding a different one that hashes to the same value.

questions.question: Why is SHA-256 vulnerable to length extension attacks, but SHA-3 is not?
options: SHA-256 uses 32-bit words while SHA-3 uses 64-bit lanes

SHA-256's Merkle-Damgård construction lets an attacker use the hash output as a chain variable and continue compression; SHA-3's sponge construction hides internal capacity bits that the attacker cannot guess
SHA-256 pads messages with the length but SHA-3 does not
SHA-3 uses more rounds (24 vs 64) making it harder to invert
questions.explanation: In Merkle-Damgård, the final hash output IS the internal chain variable. An attacker can set this as the IV and continue processing additional blocks. In SHA-3's sponge, the output comes from only the rate portion of the state, while the capacity portion (512 bits for SHA-3-256) is hidden. To extend, the attacker would need to guess those capacity bits — requiring 2^512 work.

questions.question: In the Keccak permutation used by SHA-3, which step provides the ONLY nonlinearity in the entire construction?
options: θ (theta) — column parity mixing
ρ (rho) — lane rotation
χ (chi) — bitwise mixing: x ⊕ (¬y ∧ z)
ι (iota) — round constant XOR
questions.explanation: The χ step is the only nonlinear operation in Keccak. It computes x ⊕ (¬y ∧ z), which is a 3-bit nonlinear function. All other steps (θ for diffusion, ρ for rotation, π for permutation, ι for symmetry breaking) are linear. This is a deliberate design choice: one nonlinear step per round is sufficient when combined with strong linear diffusion.

questions.question: Why is BLAKE2 faster than MD5 on modern CPUs despite MD5's simpler design?
options: BLAKE2 uses fewer rounds than MD5 (10 vs 64)
BLAKE2's ARX (Add-Rotate-XOR) operations map directly to modern SIMD instructions, while MD5's older design doesn't benefit from hardware acceleration
BLAKE2 has a smaller internal state than MD5
BLAKE2 skips message padding, saving overhead
questions.explanation: BLAKE2 uses Add-Rotate-XOR (ARX) operations that map perfectly to modern CPU SIMD units (SSE2, AVX2, NEON). MD5 was designed in 1991 for scalar CPUs — its reliance on table lookups makes it cache-sensitive and SIMD-unfriendly. On modern hardware, BLAKE2b can process data at over 1 GB/s while MD5 tops out around 400 MB/s.

questions.question: If collision resistance of SHA-256 is 128 bits (birthday bound) and preimage resistance is 256 bits, what is the security level of second preimage resistance?
options: 64 bits — weaker than both
128 bits — same as collision resistance
256 bits — same as preimage resistance
192 bits — the geometric mean
questions.explanation: Second preimage resistance requires finding a second input m₂ ≠ m₁ such that H(m₂) = H(m₁), given a specific m₁. This requires ~2^n work for an n-bit hash — the same as preimage resistance. Collision resistance is weaker (2^(n/2) birthday bound) because you get to choose BOTH inputs freely. For SHA-256: collisions cost 2^128, second preimages cost 2^256.
